title: useful functions
body:
A PPENDIX B
                  Useful Functions, Integrals and Sums


Collected here are some calculations and formulas that are espeically pertinent in probability model-
ing. We begin with several exponential integrals, the first and simplest being
                                         Z
                                            e−x dx = −e−x .                                     (B.1)

When we use integration by parts, the second integral that we introduce reduces to the first in the
manner                Z                       Z
                         xe dx = −xe + e−x dx = −e−x (1 + x).
                           −x            −x


These are the special cases α = 1 and α = 2, respectively, in the general formula valid for any real
number α for which the integrals are defined:
                       Z                                      Z
                          x e dx = −x e + (α − 1) xα−2 e−x dx.
                            α−1 −x          α−1 −x
                                                                                              (B.2)

Fixing the limits of integration leads to the Gamma function, defined by
                                        Z ∞
                               Γ(α) =        xα−1 e−x dx,    for α > 0.
                                          0

From (B.2), it follows that
                                     Γ(α) = (α − 1) × Γ(α − 1),
and therefore, for any integer k,

                              Γ(k) = (k − 1) × (k − 2) × . . . × 2 × Γ(1).

A direct consequence of (B.1) is that Γ(1) = 1, which with the above shows that the Gamma function
at integral arguments is a generalization of the factorial:

                                Γ(k) = (k − 1)!,       for k = 1, 2, 3, . . .

A more difficult integration shows that

                                                     √
                                                 
                                                 1
                                              Γ     = π.
                                                 2

Stirling’s formula is another important asymptotic evaluation of the factorial function:

                                     n! = nn e−n (2πn)1/2 er(n)/12n ,

                                   n! ∼ nn e−n (2πn)1/2               as n → ∞,                        (B.3)

the symbol “∼” signifying that the two quantities are asymptotically equivalent (their ratio approaches
